headline: Risk-On (score 2/4) — liquidity quietly improving; credit spreads and dollar both sitting just shy of their trigger thresholds
body: The regime held at Risk-On this week but it's a qualified read — only one of the three signals is fully lit. Net liquidity (the Fed's balance sheet minus money the government has parked on the sideline) ticked up to $5.90T, a +1.54% gain over the past 13 weeks. That comfortably clears the 0.5% threshold for a positive liquidity signal. The driver this week was the Treasury's General Account (TGA) falling from $876B to $828B — when Treasury draws down that account to pay bills, that cash flows back into the banking system and into markets, which is effectively a mini stimulus. The Fed's own balance sheet barely moved ($6.71T → $6.73T), and the overnight reverse repo facility (RRP) has now drained to zero. That last point is worth understanding: for the past year, money market funds had been parking hundreds of billions at the Fed overnight instead of investing it. That pool is gone. The 'easy' liquidity tailwind from RRP drainage is over; from here, net liquidity gains have to come from either the Fed buying assets again or the Treasury spending more.

High-yield credit spreads sit at 2.80% and narrowed 0.29 percentage points over 13 weeks — one basis point (0.01pp) away from triggering a formal Risk-On credit signal. Translation: the bond market is not flashing stress. Companies can borrow without paying panic-level premiums over safe government bonds. That's a constructive background for equities, even if it's not quite at the threshold that earns a formal signal. The US dollar (broad trade-weighted index) is at 120.08, up +0.67% over 13 weeks — a rising dollar is a mild headwind for non-US earnings and commodities, but the move is well below the 1.0% threshold that would flip that signal negative. Net: the backdrop is calm, not euphoric. Full risk-on would require either net liquidity to keep climbing or credit spreads to compress that last basis point. The current regime sizing hint is 'full pilot positions allowed' (3–5% starters), not 'deploy everything.'

On the GARP screen (515 large-cap stocks filtered through Peter Lynch's growth-at-a-reasonable-price criteria), 30 names passed at least 4 of 5 checks. The standout anonymized candidate before looking at names: CAND-01 in Industrials — PEG of 0.43, EPS growth of 30%, return on equity of 39%, essentially no debt (D/E 0.13), and revenue growing 19.7%. A PEG under 1.0 means you're paying less than $1 for each dollar of growth; 0.43 is deep bargain territory for a company with those ROE and growth numbers. Revealed: that's Emcor Group (EME), a large electrical and mechanical contractor riding the infrastructure and data center construction wave. The second notable is CAND-10 (IT sector, PEG 1.39, EPS growth 35.6%, revenue growth 31.5%) — Keysight Technologies (KEYS), a test-and-measurement equipment maker bouncing hard out of a semiconductor capex slowdown. CAND-06 in Energy (PEG 1.12, EPS growth 39.6%, ROE 18.2%, minimal debt) is EOG Resources — a high-quality E&P name with genuine earnings growth, not just an oil-price ride. None of these are buy signals; they're worth adding to a watchlist for deeper research.

On the 13F front (filings for Q1 2026, period ending March 31 — these lag up to 45 days, so they describe positioning from 2–3 months ago, not today): the highest cross-fund conviction plays are Taiwan Semiconductor, Amazon, and Meta, each held by 7 of the 11 tracked funds. Nvidia is in 6 funds. The most interesting fresh convergence this quarter: Broadcom was a simultaneous new buy for Druckenmiller and Third Point, while Altimeter doubled its position (+109%) — three separate conviction investors piling in during the same quarter. ASML (the Dutch monopoly that makes the extreme-ultraviolet machines used to manufacture leading-edge chips) was a new buy for Third Point, Lone Pine, and Coatue simultaneously. Visa also saw three-fund convergence: Baupost opened a new position, Lone Pine added 510% to its existing stake, and Coatue initiated fresh. Airlines are being exited across the board — Druckenmiller and Tepper both dumped airline exposure entirely. One caveat: Greenlight Capital's (Einhorn) latest available filing is from December 2023 — over two years stale — so treat that fund's data as historical only.
watch: Net liquidity at $5.897T is drifting positive, but TGA drawdowns can reverse fast. Watch whether the TGA refills above $900B next week — if it does, net liquidity drops and the regime signal weakens.

HY credit spreads at 2.80% are one basis point from flipping the credit signal to Risk-On. Watch for any move above 3.09% (the 13-week-ago level) as a regime deterioration alert; above 3.5% would be a serious warning.

The dollar at 120.08 is in neutral territory but trending up. If the broad dollar index crosses ~120.58 (1.0% above 13 weeks ago), the dollar flips to a headwind signal and the regime score drops from 2 to 1.

Broadcom convergence: Druckenmiller, Third Point, and Altimeter all added in Q1. The thesis is AI networking infrastructure. Watch Broadcom's next earnings for AI-driven revenue acceleration — if that materializes, this becomes a multi-fund thesis with real follow-through potential.
change_my_mind: Flip to stronger Risk-On: Net liquidity breaks above $6.1T (last seen in mid-2025), HY credit spreads compress below 2.51% (their 2024 tights), and the dollar rolls over below 119.00. All three signals firing simultaneously would mean high-conviction risk-on with full position sizing.

Flip to Risk-Off: HY credit spreads spike above 3.5% (the April 2025 tariff-scare peak was 4.37%), net liquidity drops more than 2% over any 4-week period (e.g. Treasury aggressively refills TGA after a debt ceiling deal), and the dollar surges above 123 on a flight-to-safety bid. Any two of those three would push the regime score negative and call for cutting pilot positions.
